response:
### Part 1: General Solution for the Population Model

The differential equation provided is:
\[
\frac{dP}{dt} = rP \left(1 - \frac{P}{K}\right) + A \sin\left(\frac{2\pi t}{12}\right)P
\]
Substituting the given values \( r = 0.3 \), \( K = 1000 \), and \( A = 0.1 \):
\[
\frac{dP}{dt} = 0.3P \left(1 - \frac{P}{1000}\right) + 0.1 \sin\left(\frac{\pi t}{6}\right)P
\]
This can be rewritten as:
\[
\frac{dP}{dt} = P \left[ 0.3 + 0.1 \sin\left(\frac{\pi t}{6}\right) - \frac{0.3}{1000}P \right]
\]
\[
\frac{dP}{dt} = P \left[ \left(0.3 + 0.1 \sin\left(\frac{\pi t}{6}\right)\right) - 0.0003P \right]
\]

This is a **Bernoulli differential equation** of the form:
\[
\frac{dP}{dt} + \left[ - \left(0.3 + 0.1 \sin\left(\frac{\pi t}{6}\right)\right) \right] P = -0.0003 P^2
\]
Or more standardly for Bernoulli:
\[
\frac{dP}{dt} - \left(0.3 + 0.1 \sin\left(\frac{\pi t}{6}\right)\right) P = -0.0003 P^2
\]

To solve this, we use the substitution \( u = P^{1-2} = P^{-1} \).
Differentiating \( u \) with respect to \( t \):
\[
\frac{du}{dt} = -P^{-2} \frac{dP}{dt} \implies \frac{dP}{dt} = -P^2 \frac{du}{dt}
